content: The study successfully developed machine-learning-optimized hydrogels (specifically R1-max) that achieve an underwater adhesive strength ($F_a$) exceeding 1 MPa on glass substrates, representing an order-of-magnitude improvement over previously reported hydrogels.
keywords: States the specific hydrogel name (R1-max)
States the quantitative achievement (> 1 MPa)
Mentions improvement over literature/previous reports

content: A specific combination of hydrophobic (BA), aromatic (PEA), and cationic (ATAC) monomers was identified as the key design principle ('Golden Triangle') for robust underwater adhesion, leveraging hydrophobic effects and electrostatic interactions.
keywords: Identifies the three key monomers: BA, PEA, ATAC
Explains the mechanism (hydrophobic + electrostatic)
States this as the core design principle

content: Ideal random copolymerization, utilizing monomers with reactivity ratios close to unity, is proven to be essential for statistically replicating protein sequence features; non-ideal synthesis leads to composition drift and significantly lower adhesive performance.
keywords: Mentions 'ideal random copolymerization'
Mentions 'reactivity ratios close to unity'
Contrasts with non-ideal synthesis/composition drift

content: Figure 1 illustrates the data-driven workflow and quantitatively compares the adhesive strength of the new DM-driven and ML-driven hydrogels against conventional literature values.
keywords: Shows the 3-step loop: Data Mining -> Experiment -> Machine Learning
Includes the comparison chart (Fig. 1b)
Visually demonstrates the gap between 'this study' and 'literature'

content: Figure 2 presents the bioinformatics analysis of 24,707 adhesive proteins, identifying 'relative composition' and specific functional class neighbors as the critical design features that guided the monomer selection.
keywords: Shows data mining results/heatmaps
Validates the 'relative composition' hypothesis
Links protein features to monomer selection

content: Figure 3 displays the screening results of the initial 180 bio-inspired hydrogels, validating that data-mining-derived formulations can yield adhesion up to 147 kPa (G-max).
keywords: Shows the bar chart/distribution of 180 samples (Fig. 3e)
Highlights the G-max performance
Demonstrates the variability in the initial dataset

content: Figure 4 demonstrates the Machine Learning optimization process, where UMAP visualization reveals that the RFR-GP model explored novel chemical spaces distinct from the training data, and SHAP analysis confirms the feature importance of BA, PEA, and ATAC.
keywords: Includes UMAP plot showing training vs. ML-generated points
Includes SHAP beeswarm plot showing feature impact
Demonstrates the 'extrapolation' capability of the model

content: Figure 5 characterizes the final super-adhesive hydrogels (R1-max), showcasing their mechanical properties, universal adhesion across diverse substrates (metals, plastics, ceramics), and exceptional durability (>1 year).
keywords: Shows stress-strain curves or mechanical data
Shows adhesion strength bar charts for multiple substrates (Fig. 5e)
Includes evidence of long-term durability (Fig. 5f)
